12 min read + 38 video
Story-Powered Promotions
(SPPs) — Part 1
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 19 --

www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 19 --

This is a three-part module.
In Part 1 we’ll share an example Story-Powered Promotion to
demonstrate what an SPP looks like.
In Part 2 we’ll discuss automation and how to set it up for SPPs
within your email service provider. Then, in Part 3, we’ll conclude
with triggers — or “doorways” — the mechanisms we use to add
people to an SPP.
Let’s get started.
Story-Powered Promotion in 48 Words:
SPP uses narrative arcs and story angles to bridge the needs and
38:26
4 Files
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 19 --

desires of a pocket of people and potential solutions within an
automated, evergreen email series comprised of 4-9 emails
triggered by active intent. Multiple SPPs, using different
arcs/angles, can be stacked for maximum effect.
Now let’s unpack this and explore each piece in detail.
Big Picture
The Revenue Engine is made of up two primary drivers: the Story-
Powered Promotion and the Product Launch Series.
In the Art of Email, SPPs are for evergreen promotions and PLSs
are for time-limited (hard open/close) promotions. Both are
triggered by active intent — a reader has to virtually ‘raise her
hand’ before she can see an SPP or PLS.
In our view of the world, this active engagement before someone
sees an offer is non-negotiable. The downstream effects from this
decision are profound.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 19 --

Details
A Story-Powered Promotion uses Principles of Serialized
Narratives to attract and sustain attention for a promotion within
the context of a larger story.
SPPs are triggered by Bridge Emails which establish the context
and create an opportunity for an engaged prospect to express
active interest.
Because SPPs are triggered by a prospect’s behavior, they’re the
perfect vehicle to make offers to audiences when the time and
context is right for them.
And, at the same time, SPPs become the workhorse of the AoE
Revenue Engine because they’re always available — 24/7/365 — to
promote to the segment of your audience that is interested in
your offer(s) right now.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 19 --

A Bridge Email creates the initial tension (framed by the ‘Hell yes!’
commitment of active intent), and that tension leads directly to
the �rst SPP email and is increased through the last email (all in
the direction of the offer that you reveal eventually in the SPP).
We’ll demonstrate this idea through an example. For context, the
framing for this email was ‘low quality leads’ (the problem),
leading to the idea that ‘better prospect are created’ (the solution).
(These emails were written in the �rst person because they
preceded Shawn’s arrival.)
Pay close attention to how you feel when you read these emails —
the experience of having your attention pulled forward using
many of the tools discussed in Module 2: Principles of Serialized
Narratives, is evident in each individual email and throughout the
entire series.
Better Prospects: Part 1 (of 3)
A few years ago, I met a subscriber at the Word Trade Center here
in Gibraltar. He was down for the day, so I agreed to meet him
(coffee on him, an irresistible offer).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 19 --

Word Trade Center, Gibraltar
He told me about an email list he had built a year earlier from
doing mobile phone giveaways (people would opt-in for a chance
of winning a new phone or tablet).
The list size was signi�cant (around 75K if I remember correctly).
His problem was monetizing it. Nothing was working. So, he
asked what I would do.
“You won’t like what I would do,” I said.
“I can handle it,” he said.
“Delete the list, move on,” I said.
“But surely there are some mass-appeal offers like weight loss or
phone insurance I can sell to them?” he suggested.
“Perhaps,” I said.
“Exactly! So what would you do?” he tried again.
“I would forget about the list and focus on attracting better
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 19 --

prospects and serving them,” I said.
“So you don’t think I can sell anything to this list?” he pushed.
“Maybe, but so what?” I said. “Even if you managed to sell some
weight loss product, then what?”
Silence.
I think he left Gibraltar disappointed after meeting me. But I also
think he learned something valuable.
This was an extreme example. Clearly, people seeking free
giveaways are not representative signals of a high-value email list.
Yet I see versions of this all the time, where an upstream behavior
to acquire (or “game”) attention, produces low-quality leads.
Thin lead capture pages that heavily rely on curiosity to capture
attention, or worse still, a big bribe like a free iPad.
Whenever (as in every single time!) I see funnels producing sub-
optimal performance, the upstream problem is activities that
produce low-quality leads.
Said another way: low-quality leads will always (always!) produce
sub-optimal performance.
But what are low-quality leads?
Well, they’re a symptom of a deeper issue, simple cause and effect,
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 19 --

really. The root cause of low-quality leads is some upstream
behavior. It always is every single time.
The solution is simple but not easy. It’s to create a process that
produces better prospects.
Simple, but not easy.
Let’s unpack this a little more.
Watch out for Part 2 tomorrow.
André
P.S.
Here’s something for you to think about between now and tomorrow.
Better prospects are made (created).
Meaning: the same person — let’s call this person Patrick — can behave
completely di�erently when he interacts with Brand A compared to Brand
B selling something similar.
Patrick buys from Brand A all the time, and he tells his friends about Brand
A on his social feeds. Yet he gives no love and attention to Brand B.
Why?
That’s the clue.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 19 --

In this example, the Bridge Email would have set up the idea that
in order to build a better email list, we need a process to create
better prospects (the hook!).
Anyone with a low-quality email list problem, will self-identify
with this problem by raising their hand (‘HELL YES!’ active
intent).
This sets up the anchor point for the tension.
The job of this �rst email was to paint a picture of a different
future reality. The tension is created — implicitly or explicitly —
between these two points (A B).
The effectiveness of this SPP is in moving someone from their
current reality, to a new future reality. Desire is channeled in the
direction — a pulling affect — of an offer that hasn’t yet been
revealed.
Counterintuitively, revealing an offer too soon will release
the tension before we’ve had a chance to build it up.
As Robert Fritz says, tension seeks resolution:
A basic principle found throughout nature is this: Tension seeks
resolution. From the spiderweb to the human body, from the formation
of the galaxies to the shifts of continents, from the swing of pendulums
to the movement of wind-up toys, tension-resolution systems are in
play.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 19 --

“I call the relationship between the vision and current reality structural
tension. During the creative process, you have an eye on where you
want to go, and you also have an eye on where you currently are.
There will always be structural tension in the beginning of the creative
process, for there will always be a discrepancy between what you want
and what you have. Why? Because creators bring into being creations
that do not yet exist. Structural tension is a fundamental principle in
the creative process. In fact, part of your job as a creator is to form this
tension.”
Our SPP is a vehicle for creating structural tension, channeling
desire, where resolution is required (our offer) in order to release
the tension.
The �rst email of an SPP is critically important, as it establishes
the frame for which the entire promotion is understood, and it
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 19 --

launches a prospect on a journey that we know — but they don’t —
will end with an offer perfectly positioned as a solution to a
problem we know they care about solving.
Let’s look at the second email of this SPP.
Better Prospects: Part 2 (of 3)
The controlling idea I revealed in the previous email is better
prospects matter. (Everything you do downstream becomes better
with better prospects.)
Poor lead quality is always a symptom of an upstream problem.
The question I left you with was how come Patrick gives a
disproportionate amount of attention to Brand A, yet Brand B,
who essentially sells the same thing, may as well be invisible?
Insight #1: All things being equal, better prospects are made
(emphasis on the ‘made’ part).
Which means you are in control of more than you think…
Here’s a scenario:
You have an ad on Facebook that gets the attention of Patrick. He
responds, ends up on your website, then onto your email list. But
for love or money, Patrick doesn’t convert to a customer.
Jeremy responds to the same ad, visits your website, ends up on
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 19 --

your email list. Then boom, just like that, he becomes a customer.
Now add scale to this scenario.
After a month of running an ad, a large cohort of prospects, like
Patrick, became leads but not customers. At the same time, same
ad, same everything, a smaller cohort of prospects, like Jeremy,
became customers.
It’s easy to jump to the conclusion that Patrick represented “bad”
targeting (FB’s algorithm, perhaps), whereas Jeremy (and the
cohort like him) was a sweet-spot match (more of them, please!).
That’s a logical conclusion.
So with logic on your side, you decide to double down and (try)
target more people like Jeremy and avoid people who represent
Patrick’s demographics or psychographics.
Simple, but not easy.
But this is a red herring.
Yes, you could get lucky and �nd more Jeremy’s than Patrick’s,
lowering your CPA (cost per acquisition) and bumping up your
AOV (average order value) a few percentage points.
But because an algorithm is a ‘black box’, even though you seem to
have control over whom you attract with your targeting (age,
interests, etc.), you don’t.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 19 --

Then like a magic trick — Abracadabra! — a month later, all of your
ads fall off the cliff. What was working, stops working. WTF!?
Re-read insight #1:
All things being equal, better prospects are made.
Remember when I said Patrick loves Brand A, but Brand B is
invisible to him?
That was the clue…
At some point, Brand A earned Patrick’s attention, and later his
trust.
It gives him an experience that elicited a positive emotional
response, so Brand A won, and Brand B was essentially made
irrelevant and mostly invisible.
Think about that.
Instead of trying to win the algorithm game, a better use of your
time (early on) is to make better prospects by leveraging what you
have full control over — the process of making better prospects.
(Note: I am not suggesting that you shouldn’t optimize how you
purchase traf�c — you should! — only that it’s a different part of
the same system and deserves its only focus in conjunction with
the work you do on the website to attract the people you want to
do business with.)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 19 --

Insight #2: You can make better prospects by what you say and do
on your website, before a prospect is added to your email list.
I’ll give you an example of what this looks like in Part 3 tomorrow.
André
P.S.
Have you ever watched a movie or read a book that completely consumed
your attention, where time almost seemed to not exist? An hour or three
disappeared in a heartbeat.
Hold on to that mental image and emotion.
Now: imagine you have a problem you desperately want to solve
(anything that matters to you that you would pay to have solved). Got it?
Now smash the two mental images and emotions together.
Done that?
What do you get?
Imagine reading about a solution to the problem you want help solving,
and it sucks you right in, consuming your attention in a good way,
shifting your worldview in some meaningful way, pulling you in wanting
to hear more?
That’s a pretty powerful feeling, right?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 19 --

Now, here’s the third example email. (Notice how tension
increases from one to the next, pulling the reader’s attention
forward in anticipation.)
That’s what I’m talking about! That emotional feeling, that experience.
Better Prospects: Part 3 (of 3)
I left you with a mental image at the end of the previous email,
where content that helped you see a meaningful problem in a new
light was so good that it pulled you along wanting more, much
like the experience you get from a good movie or book.
I want to give you some context and color to that idea with a real-
world example that is not something we’ve created.
It’s easy to share our results. But it’s altogether different when
others get results after learning a new skill.
Brooke Castillo is a life coach.
Not just a life coach by name, but a real life coach positively
affecting thousands of lives through the work she is doing.
She is a Sphere of In�uence (SOI) customer. When I chatted with
Brooke in April 2019, she said, “Our business will make $25M this
year with about half of that being the reoccurring revenue from
our membership.”
www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 19 --

The membership she referred to is the front end to her business,
called Scholars ($297/month).
Brooke went on to tell me, “I decided to buy and more importantly do
your course SOI because it was so unique and refreshing. It’s rare that I sit
and read a whole site like I did yours. I loved how much detail you were
willing to share and encourage us to do. I modeled the larger text and
white space, the way you did the photos, the hooks on each page, the
videos you popped in throughout. It feels so generous the way you create
content-making all the connections for me and giving me the option to go
deeper or simply read the shorter-less dense content.”
Before learning what we teach in SOI (and more importantly,
implementing it), Brooke had a typical frontend funnel.
“We used to do a Russell Brunson book funnel to our membership and
shut it down when we saw how much better this SOI-style site performs
for us.”
“We add about 10 new members per week on it vs 1 per week we were
doing with the book funnel. This is a HUGE accomplishment. Our
membership is $297 per month and this is to cold tra�c. It’s like
printing money. So fun!”
“(Oh! That doesn’t even mention the ones we get on our list that end up
buying 3-6 months later-which accounts for about 40% more.)”
“You’ve had a huge in�uence in the way I designed my site, wrote my
copy, and more importantly think about my business and my
customers.”
www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 19 --

Here’s Brooke’s cold traf�c SOI-style funnel:
https://thelifecoachschool.com/before/ (Please note: this link is no
longer active.)
If you resonate with her mission, you’ll feel the words pulling and
tugging at you emotionally. You’ll remember visiting her site long
after you’ve left.
External to the cold traf�c funnel above, take a look at her join
page which is accessible from her home page:
https://thelifecoachschool.com/join/
Unlike the classic MPPS (multi-page pre-sell) expression of SOI,
this one is a single short page (429 words) with a short video
(under 8 minutes). But if you read the words and listen to the
video, all the components we unpack in SOI are right there on
that page, working silently to pull and tug at her ideal customers.
Note: to be clear, we are not (repeat, not!) suggesting you’ll get the
same or similar results as Brooke when you implement what we
teach in SOI into your thinking, behavior, and actions. Brooke is
exceptional at what she does, she’s been doing this for a long time,
which likely has a disproportionate factor on her overall success.
If you want to build a better process of attracting and earning the
attention of better prospects, we welcome you to enroll in SOI.
https://tinylittlebusinesses.com/products/sphere-of-in�uence/
www.getwsodo.com

set up the automation that powers SPPs.

you’re still unsure of SOI is a good �t for you, read those emails. If

with me, she said, “Frank Kern was my coach for many years. He is also a
big fan of your work and he is currently obsessed with my new presell site
EntrepreneurialBookkeeping.com. That site is doing amazingly well
already. People are commenting on my ingenious sales letter style.”
If you have a question about SOI, let us know anytime by replying to this
email. I read every email.
